text: densely housed, the losses would have been fewer or perhaps none at all’). In October 1931, Walter Benjamin wrote a radio talk for children on the subject of the Lisbon earthquake. Here Benjamin described how the earthquake ‘excited and preoccupied the entire world like few other events in that century’ (Benjamin 2005c; here as quoted in Ray 2006: 7–8). We should understand Benjamin’s assessment well: this was the century of the French Revolution, of American Independence, and a whole series of other not-so-minor events of global significance. The Lisbon earthquake had ramifications at all levels of human life. As a single event, it shook the metaphysical optimism of Europe’s leading philosophers. According to one Voltaire scholar, the Lisbon earthquake was nothing less than ‘the death of optimism’ (Besterman as quoted in Ray: 8). Or, as Adorno put it in Negative Dialectics, ‘[t]he earthquake of Lisbon sufficed to cure Voltaire of the theodicy of Leibniz’ (1973: 361). Not without reason, Adorno invoked the earthquake under his discussion of After Auschwitz: as the Holocaust for us, in the eighteenth century it was the Lisbon earthquake which forced humans to question any easy notion of progress and optimism. Even the material effects were rapidly felt all over Europe, and they affected the economy hugely. But much more fundamentally: it affected the whole conception of how humans should think and act in a world in which such events occurred. It forced rational thought to deal with liminality. And it did so in its own very peculiar and problematic way – and this is best seen in the work of Kant. The Lisbon Earthquake and the Liminal Sources of Modern Philosophy: The Impact on Immanuel Kant Kant was twenty-nine years old when the earthquake occurred. He was a young man who had never left his hometown of Königsberg; socially challenged as he was, he would never do so in the future. It might be said that the earthquake was the one pivotal event that brought the world to Kant in all its drama, and against which he reacted by mobilizing his cognitive powers. It must be remembered that Kant’s first publication (which for reasons still under debate he did not submit as his Master’s thesis, and rather led him to drop out) was on ‘living forces’, Thoughts on the True Estimation of Living Forces. Kant’s motivating question in his early career (and it stayed with him even as it altered) was to understand what moves things in nature: which ‘forces’ are in play, how substances interact, and how we can observe and study these forces. In this work, Kant systematically and dissectingly discussed the question of natural forces and their interactions. He was interested in dynamic forces, and in what happens when forces meet and collide, with effects back to the original activity. The book was a failure; no-one took notice of it, and his critique of Descartes and Newton went largely unnoticed. Kant went for it again with a 1754 publication, where he returned with the more famous Spin-Cycle essay. As its title states, this work is an ‘investigation of the question whether the earth in its axial rotation, Dimensions of Liminality 97 whereby it causes the change of day and night, has experienced any change since the earliest times of its origin, and how one could answer this question’. Kant here describes gravitational attraction as ‘nature’s universal engine’ (das allgemeine Triebwerk der Natur). In the essay, Kant also announces his next book (1755) with the working title, ‘Cosmogony, or attempt to deduce the origin of the cosmos, the constitution of celestial bodies, and the causes of their motions, from the general laws of motion of matter according to Newton’s theory’ (Schönfeld 2012). Cosmic action turns on gravitation, the reciprocal attraction of masses. When drawn together, masses collide, crash, and are laterally deflected. The angular momentum of deflections generates a counterforce to centripetal gravitation: centrifugal repulsion. In his second book Universal Natural History and Theory of the Sky (1755), Kant sees that the pumps of the entelechy, its push and pull, are attraction and repulsion. Matter is, then, all you need, he says, ‘and you can start building a world’ (ibid.). ‘Start building a world’ – this is very much what Kant’s thought is all about, and it is this notion that later comes to underpin social constructivism. Kant’s theory also prepared the ground for today’s Big Bang theories. The pulse of the bond, Kant would move on to argue, a flaring out structure, is best imagined as fire, one of nature’s essences, and hence a component of the cosmic matrix. In fact, Kant eventually graduated with A Succinct Outline of Some Meditations on Fire, a penetration into the structure of fire. Cosmic forces, gravity, earthly rotation, interaction of elements, space-time collusion, small and big bangs, entropy, fire: those were Kant’s foundational philosophical interests. The Lisbon earthquake had it all. It erupted at the ‘right moment’, in one of Kant’s most dynamic phases, just as he was attempting to bridge his natural philosophy with a newly grounded epistemology. After his Master’s, Kant wrote his dissertation, the New Elucidation of the First Principles of Metaphysical Cognition (1755). It is no longer about force, cosmos, or fire, but about ontology, specifically the conceptual tools for metaphysical investigations. This became Kant’s ultimate motive: to explore the cognitive access to nature, the causal structure of human integration into nature. The earthquake therefore erupted right between Kant’s dissertation and his habilitationsschrift, the Physical Monadology (1756), when Kant was still working on the Physical Monadology, and just three weeks after he had given his very first lecture (which he delivered on 13 October). Kant’s Writings on the Lisbon Earthquake Kant published three essays on the catastrophe in 1756. The first, On the Causes of the Terrestrial Convulsions (January 1756), was an essay on the physical dynamics of earthquakes.
